Hey guys, JB is in the house. Time for the weekly intensive training and as promised, this week's topic is going to be on tonality, but I'm also going to talk about overall unconscious communication. And the reason for that is because if you guys, if you're on the daily motivations, you probably noticed one of my videos this week, one of my trainings was a guy named Chancey who works for me, right? And I kind of had this little rip into shred session with his body language where he went to a seminar and this is why, you know, when someone doesn't really know what they're doing and they're teaching, it's very dangerous. He went to a seminar, I don't want to name the company, but they're obviously a bunch of idiots because they gave him knowledge without giving him the context of how to use the knowledge. And also the knowledge they gave was kind of stupid and had to do with dressing for a success. And that classic mistake they made was they equated dressing with six for success with wearing a suit and tying. And that's just not nothing to do with dressing for success is, you know, dressing up in a suit. Now in some industries, yes, that is dressing for success. But in most industries, it's not. All right. So I kind of went and kind of ripped him apart on camera. This was, I think on Wednesday, maybe a Tuesday or Wednesday of last month, this week, right? And then I went out and got him a new suit, you know, got him a new shirt, the whole thing. And he showed how you could take someone from like just looking weird and, you know, off putting to the point where you're never going to do business with them. However, with, you know, a basic quick makeover, it took us like 15 minutes, right? The guy actually looks really, really good. All right. And maybe think that, you know, today, I don't want to just talk about tonality per se. I want to talk about tonality in the context of unconscious communication. So let me explain to you, I'm going to start from the beginning here, okay? And really explain, you know, unconscious communication versus conscious communication, how they work together. And also how you can quickly get to the point where you can use unconscious communication to, you know, dramatically increase your closing rate like right now, very quickly, right? So let me quickly explain why I talk about unconscious communication unconscious, right? Versus just conscious communication. When we talk about conscious, we typically think of it's the words that we say. It's the words, it's the logical case that we make. It's how we get our point across to someone in their conscious mind. In other words, remember that the human brain is really, really powerful, right? It's immensely powerful. But our unconscious mind is infinitely more powerful than our conscious mind. Our conscious mind is that part of our brain that tells us what we should focus on, what's important. And then it can really capture maybe three to four percent of what's going on around us. For instance, right now, you're listening to the words that I say, right? Your eyes are seeing my face, you're zeroing it on me, right? Maybe depending on where you're sitting, if there's a very loud noise around you, you hear that, okay? If someone else is talking loudly, you'll hear that, right? If it's particularly cold or warm where you are, you'll notice that it's warm or cold, right? But out, and maybe there's something in the background that's like right in your field of vision that you can't ignore. But what typically happens is, okay, is that because the conscious mind has a limited amount of processing power, it essentially deletes out everything that it sees, hears, touch, taste and feel your five senses, right? It deletes it out so it's able to manage and make sense of what's important around you. In other words, your brain has dictated that, you know what? What's important right now is I need to understand exactly what Jordan's saying and to think about those words, internalize those words. I want to watch him pick up his body language shoes, the whole thing. That's a conscious decision you've made, right? You're sitting down, hopefully your chair is comfortable. So after a couple of minutes of sitting, you're no longer aware even of the pressure that the chair is exerting on your legs, but it's still there. You're no longer aware of the temperature of the feeling of the air on your skin. You don't notice any of the lights that are on that you're sort of part of the background. In other words, what your brain does because your conscious mind has limited processing power, it makes a decision and that decision comes from a place called your RAS, your particular activating system. And this is that part of your brain that chooses what you should focus on. Okay. So right now you're focusing on me and just a couple other things. The rest of the information from coming in from your five senses, your conscious mind says, not important enough. I'm going to ignore that. However, it doesn't get lost. You see, while your conscious mind has a relatively limited amount of processing power, your unconscious mind has unlimited processing power. It can capture everything. I want you to think of this way. Imagine like almost like your, your conscious mind, your RAM, your random access memory. It's what you can do right now to do tasks. It's like task oriented in the moment situation. So while you might have only a little amount of RAM on your computer, you could have a massive hard drive that stores all the information. That's a pretty good analogy of how the brain works. So while you are not consciously aware of every little thing around you, your unconscious mind picks it all up. It's literally, it's like millions of times more powerful than you are conscious. So watch. So while you're listening to me, your unconscious mind is busy working away over time, regulating your heartbeat, controlling your breathing, making sure that your blood vessels are delivering enough oxygen to the different parts of your body, making sure that your digestion system is functioning correctly. So there's everything, all the autonomic functions in your body are completely being regulated without you even thinking about it. In other words, the conscious mind is what you're thinking about doing. I'm, I'm focusing on Jordan conscious decision. Your unconscious mind takes care of everything else. Make sense? Good. Now here's the deal. That into itself is not that profound. However, what makes this profound is when you start to realize that while you delete all this other information, your unconscious mind does not only capture it all, but it also organizes it and uses develop patterns of behavior. In other words, if I right now I'm going to go walk out there's a door right there, right? When I walk out that door, right? I open the door and I simply step out, right? I've seen that door many, many times, right? Let's say I go to a strange door, door I've never seen before. I don't walk up to the door and say, hmm, that's a door. I wonder what's on the other side. Is it safe to go outside or is it a parallel universe outside? Is it going to be a tiger? My unconscious mind tells me based on your life experience and entering and exiting through doors that there's going to be a safe zone on the other side. It's simply a way for me to get from one room to another from inside to outside. Without my unconscious mind, I would have to stop at every single door, every crack in the sidewalk. Everything I do would have to be for the first time. My unconscious mind turns things into patterns which allows me to move through the world elegantly. You get the difference? So in those I don't look at a door and say, hmm, what is that strange little mob there? I wonder what happens if I've never seen this particular knob before. I wonder what this knob does. No, my brain says, okay, this is a knob. You've seen four billion knobs throughout your life. This knob needs to be turned. It's not like every knob is the first knob I saw. Guess what? All of that stuff, all those patterns, all of that instant decision making, you don't think about it all comes from your unconscious mind. That's what all this is. You don't walk up to a door and say, let me see if I should make it. Should I open this door or not? No, it's instantaneous. Your unconscious mind, it relieves your conscious mind of the task. So because it doesn't have to worry about all that shit, now your conscious mind can focus on what's important, that three or four percent that really, really matters. Like when you walk out that door, what exactly do you see? Okay, so your conscious mind is basically what's in front of you, what your focus is on, your unconscious mind takes in everything else and turns it into patterns of behavior that you can then act on. So when we talk about someone having a very high action threshold, we're talking about the beliefs they have about making buying decisions, that I make good decisions quickly, or I should always think about things. That's another set of beliefs. Or I should trust, in general, I should trust salesman. Or salesman or not to be trusted. Those are patterns that your unconscious mind has developed over years and years based on everything that you've seen, everything that you've heard, whether it's on TV in person, from a distance up close, it could be something as simple as you're watching, you're reading something else on TV in the corner. There was something about salesman being dishonest and some funny gag on TV about salesman not to be trusted. You could have been watching Glenn Gary, Glenn Ross, by the way, right? When they, you know, it's all about salesman not to be trusted in their hard charging salesman, even the Wolf of Wall Street. And you could have been a kid and that picked up something, got inserted in your unconscious mind and was the seed for not trusting salespeople. It could be something your parents whispered in your ear when you were six years old. It could be your brain saying, don't take care, your mommy said, don't take candy from strangers, you're getting the call with someone you don't know. And your unconscious mind has it extrapolated that and turned it into one giant pattern of don't trust anyone. See, what very often happens with our unconscious mind is that it develops patterns while we're very young. And those patterns serve us while we're young. They protect us. They help us move through the world without getting in trouble, without, you know, walking off a cliff, without getting into the car with a potentially bad person, right? They protect us, not to take candy from strangers, right? The whole thing. But as an adult, the rules of the game change. See, now you're educated enough that your conscious mind is, has enough experience that, you know, you kind of know when you see something, if it makes sense or not, that's conscious, does it make sense or not? And you should act on it. Yet your unconscious mind is so powerful that it instantly predisposes you towards not taking action. This is why so many people have problems achieving success. It's not cause the I will join my programs in the early days, I would go out there, I teach people how to close, how to sell all the stuff you need to be successful on a sales side, right? I didn't teach him entrepreneurship, marketing, how to run a business. But I didn't address their belief systems. I didn't address the unconscious, their inner game. And because of that, some of them didn't achieve success. When I realized that, I said, Oh my God, I, if I really want to help people achieve massive success, I have to speak to both of their minds. I can't just teach someone a skill without essentially addressing the emotional baggage that is emotional baggage. They have in terms of patterns that don't serve them anymore. Like the parents said, money is the root of all evil. Or if you work really hard, you're going to ignore your family or you lose your relationship with God, or people won't like you. People will resent you, you lose your friends. There's all of these things get inserted in us when we're very young. And they form these patterns of behavior. And then they either serve us through our life and protect us, or they plague us. They stop us from taking action. They stop us from charging forward. And most importantly, now, to bring it back to sales and persuasion, they're in every person you speak to, every single person you try to influence, every person you negotiate with, everyone you try to close, they are all being controlled by these unconscious patterns of behavior. Their unconscious mind gives them gut feels either are going to be, yeah, I want to do it or not. I don't trust sales people. I got to think about things. So much of the objections that we hear. And the reticence that people have to taking actions they should be taking. Remember, I said this is a great clip that I say, you know, it's about getting this is a listen, the straight line is powerful. And when you become proficient at it, you can get people to do things they shouldn't do and buy things they shouldn't buy. And I say, don't do that. I'm serious, don't do that. What you want to use the straight line for is to help people do things they should be doing, take actions they should be taking by things they should be buying. But they're blocked in some way because of these patterns of behavior. So the patterns block them from getting the things they need and living the life they deserve. As a salesperson using the straight line system, by addressing the unconscious aspect of communication as well, we allow these people to get the things they need, the things that they want, things that will make their life better by helping them overcome those unconscious barriers and obstacles that exist in their mind. And they're not even fucking aware of them, they're taking action. That's why I'm able to close at such a high level. Because intuitively, when I speak, when I influence, I'm always speaking to both minds, the unconscious mind and the conscious mind. So the goal for today here, and I think that I'm going to take my chance to get on, finally it's getting hot in here, so it's still cold here for the last few weeks, right? Finally getting some good weather here. So here's the deal, right? The goal for today is to, number one, kind of illuminate for you what's really going on when you're sitting in front of a prospect, talking to them on the phone or trying to get them to click on a button you have said, why do they do it? Why don't they do it? And then how do we use today? We're going to focus on this asset. How do we use unconscious communication to dramatically tip the odds of success in our favor? That's the frame for today, okay? So that being said, let's talk about how we speak to both minds. So when it comes to the conscious mind, our primary way of communicating with the conscious mind is through words. We say things that make an airtight logical case, hopefully, right? Or some sort of logical case or attempt to make a logical case in order to show someone that what we're selling has value. So for instance, let's say I'm selling insurance, right? And I want to sell someone a life insurance policy. The conscious case that I make is that this policy makes sense for you to buy economically. You put an X number of dollars, right, which is a very low amount based on what you make as an inconsequential amount. And on the outside, the other side of the equation, if something goes wrong, God forbid, right, you get X number of dollars back, and that money will then cover this expense, that expense and set the people that you love up for the future. So essentially, make sense. But that's not enough. It's also better than the competition, meaning based on what other people charge for that same service, ours is a better, there's a cost benefit ratio, we have better features, we have better cancellation features, we allow you to borrow against it. In other words, it's the A, B, C. It's the words that you say. It's the case that you make about cost benefit ratio, the actual features and the benefits, logical certainty, the airtight logical case comes from the words that we say. And on the flip side of the equation, we have the unconscious communication that comes from our tonality and our body language. Now, the subject of today is specifically tonality, but I don't want to just go through the 10 tonalities. I've done that before and I'll touch on them and I'll keep doing that. We just keep doing it again and again. This is about constant, never ending improvement, right? But the point is I want to give you a different perspective that's going to allow you to really understand what's going on and why this is so important. For instance, the other day, I was training someone in my own company who doesn't have sales experience, not, and he does not use a straight line. And I watched the guy, I heard the guy at the corner of my selling and it's like fingernails on a blackboard to me, right? And I'm saying, all right, well, what's so terrible? Well, number one, you know, the words were wrong. I caught his first phrase, the words were not correct, right? And even worse than that, because the words were incorrect, the tonality being applied, it was just so off that it just sounded horrific to me. And I realized that it's not just enough to explain someone the logical side, like it's important because you need to make this case. There's something else going on. And what it is is that when the words are in the correct order, it starts to make sense why you're applying certain tonalities. I always say, if you go back to the rules of prospecting, I say, one of the rules is every question you ask has its own specific tonality, a best tonality. And if you apply the right tonality, you get one response. And if you apply the wrong tonality, you get a very different response, right? And I watched the time and I said, you know what, I need to go back a step here and really explain why this is so crucial. So what happens is the words, right, are making the airtight logical case, and your tonality and body language in person are speaking to the person's unconscious mind and hitting those patterns of behavior and giving them gut feelings about whether something makes sense or not. Should I keep listening or not? Can this person help me or not? It is not a logical decision they make. It's just they get a gut feeling and we call that a gut feeling, but it happens up here based on everything you've seen, heard, read, experienced, whether it was directly or out of the corner of your eye, it forms these patterns and how you dress, how you speak, how you shake hands, how you make eye contact, either it serves to say, yep, this pattern says it's a person worth listening to, or the pattern says there's something going wrong here. I got to get out of the situation quick. So when I saw this guy work, Chancey, right, who's an intern here, right? And I see this kid and I take one look at him. I'm like, I'm like, there's something wrong there. The words, no matter what he said, he would never have a chance to show his stuff, because my unconscious mind put out a warning bell and it said novice alert, novice alert, not his person does not know what's going on, novice alert, don't talk, you get it? So when it comes to unconscious communication, it's a combination of how we dress, how we make eye contact, how we shake hands, how we move our body, how close we stand, the general feeling someone gets when they lay eyes on us for the first time. And the tonality is what we apply to the words that we say. Okay, so and together those things make up this body of knowledge unconscious communication. When you think of tonality, and here's now where it gets interesting, mostly we talk about using tonality to keep someone interested, to keep someone hanging on the edge of their seat, to actually get our point across to speak to someone emotionally. So we know tonality is about creating emotion. So I said to you, watch, hey, John, the reason for the cause, hey, John, the reason for the cause. Or I say to you, John, this product is awesome, it's going to fill your needs, it will help you resolve all your problems. I said, John, this problem, this will help you fill your needs, it will resolve all your problems. I say it one way, whatever, I say it the other way, it has a very different effect. Right? So that's the basic form of tonality, right? You know, we say something and it makes gives an emotional response. But there's a much, much higher level of tonality that I want to go through right now. It's got nothing to do with just the emotions got to do with taking control. And you've heard me say this many times about taking control of someone's monologue, their internal monologue. And this is basically the key to the whole thing. When you speak to someone, they look at you. And at the same time they look at you, they're like, uh-huh, uh-huh, their brain saying that makes sense. This is stupid. This guy's a moron. Oh, wow, this guy's really sharp. Oh, that's good. Oh, I want to hear more. They talk to themselves. People will the brain multitasks, right? So remember, as you speak to someone, whether it's in person or over the phone, their brain is running away a mile a minute, narrating either for you against you. It's a silent narration that's running in the background. And what happens is unless you can take control of that internal narrative, it will start narrating heavily against you because the patterns that we've all developed since we're a kid are, don't trust, what does this person want to get from me? What do they want? What's their angle? This is how we're all built. Unless you're a really naive person, okay? There's that, even me with a low action, so there is that side of you that's always listening. Hmm, let me think. What's this person to okay, oh my god, what do they want to get in the air? Sure. I narrate. You can't help yourself unless the person uses a very high level of straight line communication. You see what happens when you use the straight line at a really high level and you're layering on tonality at the tonality at the tonality and in person body language, what ends up happening is you lock up someone's unconscious monologue that inner monologue. You lock it up and you take control of it. This is, by the way, this is when you're closing at the super high level. Let me repeat that because there's kind of a difficult concept. Does someone, every human being, imagine your prospect, right? They have an unconscious monologue, right? So you're talking, saying, hey John, how are you doing today? They're like, oh my god, it's fucking salesman. What does this person want? And they say, okay, good. So they're talking to you and they're smiling and they're answering, but their brain inside is saying something different to themselves. They're trying, what's his angle? What's he's trying to say? What does that mean? What did he mean? Okay, that made sense. That didn't make sense. He's an asshole. Oh wow, he's really smart. I wonder what's going on with this TV show. They're like, they can have five things going on in their head as you're trying to influence them. But when you start applying the straight line tonalities, one after the next, after the next, what ends up happening is you take control of someone's unconscious monologue because you essentially lock it up. Watch what happens. Okay, it starts with literally the first words out of your mouth. So your intro, right? The reason I phrase my declarative, I phrase declarative as a question. This is one of your tonalities, one of the 10 tonalities. What does that mean? So when I start awful conversation, I say, hi, my name is Jordan Belford calling from XYZ company. Now watch again. Hi, watch ready? Hi, my name is Jordan Belford. I'm calling from XYZ company in New York. How are you today? And when I ask a question, I raise my voice and think, how are you today? And I go, that's just how we communicate in this language and every language has a very similar pattern. So we say our declarations one way, we make statements one way and we ask questions another way. I don't say, how are you doing today? How are you doing today? I use a question like tonality. So when I say, Hey, my name is Jordan Belfort. That is the expected way. That's the expect. Hi, my name is Jordan Belfort statement calling from XYZ company statement in Los Angeles. And I say, how are you today? That's the expected tonality. One will hear what they think they'll hear right when you pick up the when you when you answer the phone. So because expected, the what happened is the unconscious mind. Okay, is like, Oh my God, here we go. So what's going on? Another salesman. Oh my God, right? But when you flip this upside down on its head, and you phrase a declarative as a question, here's what happens. It now looks like this. Hi, my name is Jordan Belfort. Hey, it's Jordan Belfort calling from XYZ company in New York. How you doing today? Uptown, uptown, they're like, what? Who is this? It sends their mind into search mode. So watch what's there. So not really think this through for a second. When someone raises their voice like that, and speaks in question like tones, what would you be thinking? You think, who do I know this person? Let me go Jordan Belfort. Does that ring the bell? Well, and then going even deeper, all the stuff that you don't actually say, but your unconscious mind is collapsing as well. I would hate to be out of the loop here because that would make me look stupid. Or I know it's impolite because my mother told me I'm supposed to remember people I speak to more than once. And obviously, based on what this guy just said, using that tonality, I guess I'm supposed to know this guy. All of that. It's all reverberating in someone's brain. Seriously. That is precisely what happens when you phrase something as a question in the beginning. It's supposed to be a statement. They're like, what? That's what happens. Okay. So what do they do now? Instead of saying to themselves, oh, Jesus, another salesman, what the fuck does this guy want? What is this shit about? They're like, say to themselves, yeah, okay, do I know this person? So they'll say, so you say, how you doing today? They'll be like, I'm okay. Now he doesn't now watch this unconscious mind says, hmm, that green doesn't match the pattern I'm used to the pattern of greetings I have is a disingenuous greeting that my mother taught me to make my father told me to make where I say, how are you today? So what's what you're doing is you're essentially you're breaking their pattern again. They're like, they're saying, wait a second, hey, they're like, I'm okay. Like this person seems to really care. The toilet seat, I care. So you have two tone hours phrasing it to clarify the question. And then the I really want to know the idea how you doing? I really want to know. And they're like, I'm okay. So what's happened? They're like, what's going on? And I say, okay, great. Now, the reason for the call today. And now I lower my voice and like, oh, you like, there's a secret going on a month. My mind might I was taught my whole life that when someone lowers their voice, the information is very important. It's in short supply. And it's some scarcity information. And I better find out what this does. It could be really important. That's why he's lowering his voice. This is what happens. It's all unconscious. They're not saying those words to themselves. They're unconscious mind that has been developed over x 30 years, 40 years, 20 years of life, is throwing out all this stuff into their conscious mind. And what happens is tonality, tonality, tonality. Guess what they're not doing. They're not narrating against you anymore. What they're doing instead now is they're trying to keep up with all the unspoken words they're hearing for your tonality. In other words, you say how you doing today. Those are a few words. But by saying how you doing today, the words that they also hear are I really want to know. They hear extra words. Like when you say not trying the reason for the call. And what you've also said was now, John, this is really important. You get that. Listen to this, John. Don't tell anyone. They cannot help but get those feelings inside from tonality because your tonality is not speaking to this part of their mind. That's not who you're speaking to. You're speaking to this part of the person's mind. You speak to their unconscious mind and their unconscious mind is made up of strictly patterns. It's not logic. It's patterns, snap reactions, gut feelings, instant decisions. While your words are being analyzed by their conscious mind, which has very little processing power relatively. So all they can think about consciously, right, is that, okay, I'm hearing what he said and I speak this language, right? So I'm okay. Yeah. And any extra bit of processing power left is completely obliterated by the extra words they're hearing and the thoughts and feelings they have based on the patterns you've hit. So you're addressing their patterns with your unconscious communication. So it's double communication. You're communicating consciously and unconsciously at the same time, using your words to speak to their conscious mind and your tonality and body language to speak to their unconscious mind. This is why we look at certain people and we just want to, I want to do business with them. I like them. They mix. It's a pattern based on what I've seen throughout my life. The chances are really high that if someone that looks like this, talks like this and acts like this, it's probably so worth listening to. So we tip the odds dramatically in our favor by focusing on our unconscious communication as well as our conscious communication. We focus on both. So let's keep going here. So in the first example, right, you said, hi, my name is Jordan Belfort, calling from XYZ company in New York. How are you doing today? Declaration, declaration, or how are you doing today? And they're like, I know that you know that you know that I know that you don't give a shit. It's just a perfunctory greeting. It means nothing. That's one way versus, hey, it's Jordan Belfort calling from XYZ company in LA. How you doing today? And they're like, okay, good. And you're like, great. Now, the reason for the call today, okay, is something just came across my desk and that you get it. And what's happening is they're in your magnetic zone, rather than saying, oh, shit, they're hanging on your every word. And that's what we're doing when we control someone's unconscious monologue. It stops their, their essentially unconscious mind from creating this inner monologue that says this guy's a moron, don't trust a salesperson. And it's powerful. But there's this higher purpose that's, that's essentially taking place in the background, running through the whole sale. There's two sales going on. There's the sale to the conscious mind and there's a sale to the unconscious mind. There's a reason why I developed the system this way, because it allows you to speak to both minds at once. That makes sense. And I want to really give you some more context about how they get used effectively. And I want to focus on one. I want, I'm going to start in a different order than usual, because there's a tonality that I use myself all the time. And I didn't realize it, like, because, you know, it's so unconscious for me. I do it, that, you know, someone that worked who's actually behind the camera here, Dan, who's running the Q&A, right. So Dan, I was on the phone with someone in a, you know, negotiating a deal. And I humped the phone, Dan starts laughing. I'm like, what's so funny? Because like, do you know, do you realize what you're doing, like with all the straight line? I'm like, well, I guess so. I mean, you're like, dude, you're running like these perfect straight line patterns. And like your presupposing tonalities, like you can be using that again, I can't believe how powerful it is. Like it sounds so good. Like this is one tonality. And it's, I'm so glad he says it, because it's like, it's almost like the throwaway tonality, people think it's cool. I think it's number eight or nine, we'll get to it, right? But I'll go, I'm going to go through it first. It's when it gets neglected, this tonality, right? It's called, let's use number nine. It's called your presupposing tone. It's when you're in the latter part of a sale and you, you're making your airtight logical case. And let's say it's going to be that, let's say you're trying to, let's, let's use the seminar world, right? Because it was in the case of a seminar world. And so when I was explaining to someone how, listen, so, you know, we're going to do this whole thing and, you know, you can sell the tickets and you'll sell out the room, right? But on top of that, there's also this other stuff happening because these people love to buy products and there's money to be made there. And there's, we can come back down the road and do even more and develop code. So I was trying to, so the initial sale was with someone who wants to do a live event with me in a certain country. Let's just pick, let's just pick Singapore. Okay. So I want to do a live Jordan Belford event in Singapore, right? So when someone is talk, when a promoter is talking to me about that, right, what's their big concern? Well, if I do a live event in Singapore, I want to make sure that I sell out the room, right? That's what I'm going to talk about that, right? Yeah. Well, if you, if you pay me my fee and you advertise my name because it's the brand and because people need to learn how to sell, you'll be able to sell enough tickets, not just break even, you'll make a lot of money, right? But what I end up doing is I go way beyond just taking that conversation about, yeah, you'll fill up the room. What I saw say is, do you, you'll listen, obviously you'll fill up the room, but beyond that. So I use this tone out and I'm like, yeah, obviously, yeah, we'll fill the room up. But more importantly, what happens beyond that is all the products that people are going to buy going back year after year, you're building a business for the long term that can really make you rich. In other words, what I've done is I talk about the events in like, it's a foregone conclusion. I say, you'll make money with the event, but besides that, on top of that, it's much bigger than that. That's just the beginning. Like, so what I, by doing that, I essentially take the question of success or failure out of the picture for gong. So I say, yeah, yeah, you'll fill the room. But more importantly, let's talk about the ancillary benefits. You'll also get this, you'll get that, you'll get that. That's your presupposing tone. And I guess, you know, until Dan pointed that out, I was like, it's so in second digit, Mike, he kept saying, dude, it was so amazing. Like, I want like, you could just see how the conversation was like, you, it was like, you, you went, you're like, you will check, like, check, it's done, like, it's over. It's a success. You took it, you future pace the conversation past what you were even selling the guy and say, okay, you already bought that you succeeded. Now we're talking about what happens next. And that presupposing tone, which is ignored by most people is so fucking powerful. Because remember what you've done here is like, I, I, I said, I said, yeah, you know, yeah, listen, and you will, you'll fill the event up. That's not even the issue here. Let's talk past that. But remember, what was the conversation about? His concern is, if I do this, am I going to fill the room? I'm like, you'll fill the room book. Forget that. That's the easy part. Let's talk about how you make real money over the long term. And I moved the conversation past what I'm even selling the person using this presupposing tone. That makes sense. This is really fucking powerful guys. Okay. Now you don't want to use this on ethically. You want to use it. So, you know, you don't just say the beginning, oh, if I, if I said the beginning, it wouldn't make sense. But what I had done is I spent the first part of the sale making the presentation, making this air tight logical case. So it all made sense. It all, it was already, it was already there. He said, yeah, it makes, he's already basically, I like it. It makes sense. I see how I can make money. And then once I have him to that place of, well, yeah, it logically makes sense. I then do this leapfrog. I go, yeah, you'll make money with that. Let's talk about what, let's talk about the real stuff. This is easy. Let's talk about the really make money over the long term coaching program. And I'm dead serious. Like I'm not making any of this up. In other words, I'm not lying to the guy. I'm not trying to get over on the guy. It's a strategy of communication where I build up this really solid case in the beginning logically. So I satisfy his conscious mind. All right. And then I use this presuppose. Oh, yeah, you'll make money with a bone more importantly, what else you can do. Something just came up. So there's a bit of excitement, but you'll lower your voice and that's the tonality of scarcity. Okay. And remember this three times, you have the words is called verbal scarcity, right? John, this is in very short supply. That's verbal. The words John, this is in very short supply or total scarcity and on to John, this is in very short supply. You add the tonality of scarcity onto the words. And then you have informational scarcity, which is John, this is in very short supply and no one even knows it yet. Even the information is scarce. Those are your three levels of scarcity. Okay. Next, you have the tonality of I really want to know. That's just basically what I call I really want to know, right? So I said, you John, how you doing today? Not how you're doing today. So I said to you, how you doing today? You're like, you know, that I know, that I know that you know, that I don't give a shit how you're doing today. And you don't care how I'm doing. They're like, okay, I'm what the fuck's going on? And you own them. And I don't mean that in a bad way. I don't mean that you hurt them to get out of there, but you're in your magnetic zone. So there's without that, without this, what happens? The person you can't, they shut down. They try to take control of a conversation. And once that happens, guess what? You can't run any patterns. Mystery and intrigue is that what you say to someone and by the way, I used it as an example the reason for the call. That's when you have that reason you're hanging with that R, you're rolling the reason for the call. So mystery, you're creating intrigue. And what it does, it makes them press the phone closer. They just want to know. It's like not quite scarcity. The reason like, you know, so I sort of divide the scarcity urgency, right? So scarcity is about urgency, mystery and intrigue is about keeping them interested, hanging on your every word. Okay. And we use these 10 tonalities, not just to keep them interested as we talk. Remember, in the beginning, especially it's about capturing their attention, taking control of their unconscious monologue, stopping it from narrating against you and get it to narrate for you. When you can do that, okay, it's easy to do. When you do that, you'll notice that everything else becomes easy because they stop saying, Oh, fuck, watch this, who's this? They stop acting like assholes and start treating you with the respect you deserve. That's the key. And with that, now let's open up to our questions and let's rock and roll. So, so, so in, if you, um, if you read the book, way of the wolf, you probably have, you just forgot, right? In, in way of the wolf, I talk about this whole thing about the difference to the two-call system in a one-call system is that in a two-call system, you're entering into the conversation a second time, you're further down the straight line and you already have some gathered some intelligence, right? So what you want to do is you, a second call starts with like, Hey, John, if you recall, we spoke last week, I sent you out some information via email, okay, that ring a bell, not did you receive it? Does that ring a bell? So I'll say, no, I didn't receive it. And then you start with a no. I say, well, the reason for the call today, John, okay, is whatever it is, right? And then I'll say, okay, real quick, let me just, that last time we spoke and I'll quickly go through the, I won't go through all the questions. I go through a very, very, an abbreviated intelligence gathering. I just sort of confirm what they said last time and use that to get back into tight rapport again, right? Because remember, when you ask those questions the first time, you use it as an opportunity to get into deep rapport, through active listening. So what I'll do now is I go through those questions again, but very quickly. And I'm like, aha, you're up. And I said, now, let's we spoke, you said that XYZ was that, is that right? Right? He says, yeah, right. Aha, and he'll start talking again. And he uses to get back into rapport. But a second call will typically imagine, let's say, your, your initial call was six minutes in a two call system to get to the point, he said, great, let me send you out the information and we'll meet again, right? But we'll talk again. If it was six minutes in your opening call, it should be two and a half to three minutes in your second call. So you condense that portion, use it to get back into tight rapport, and then go into your presentation. Okay, that's the answer. So this is from Peter. I'm a house painter, does tonale work when you are talking to a client that tells you that from the beginning he's getting three quotes? I keep hearing this, okay, this whole issue about like multiple quotes and does the straight line. And here's the deal, buddy. Okay, if everyone is quoting $98, you're quoting $150, there's no tonality in the world is going to save the day. Flat out. The straight line works when everyone's quoting around the same price, and they want to go with you because of all the other intangibles. So maybe the straight line system and influence and persuasion, maybe they'll give you a 10%, 20% leeway. So if your job is 100, and everyone's charging 100, you're charging 175, there's no tonality in the world that's going to get you that job, buddy. Unless they don't care about money. We're talking about when you're all around the same price, and you use your tonality, your buy-in, and also the words that you say to make sure that your service stands out and they trust you and they trust the company that stands behind you. That's what this is. So it's not like this magic solution for charging extra. We just be clear about that. But I promise you that if you have every, if it's three or four people around the same price, even your $5 more, whatever it might be, you use the right tonality, buy it, use your right, not just that, everything, the straight line, you will be the winner. All right. So keeping the powder dry is about keeping the ammunition for a position track. Partially, partially my friend, keeping your powder dry has to do with many things. Let's do number one, that there's only so much you can move someone in any one shot, like you know when you have a car and you have gears, it goes, and it has that back off where like the car's going to stop, pause, and then go forward. Someone doesn't go from a four to a 10 in one shot. If you try to go from a, take them from a four to a 10, all they do is get overwhelmed. Someone can only take so much information at once. So you frame your case, you keep your powder dry, that's one side of it. Another side is that what I don't want to do is get, say, everything about my product and service, right? And then I'm done, and they want to think about it, and I've left myself with nothing great to change their models. What I'd rather do is I, I'm not saying don't make an amazing case in the first go. Do you believe in yourself? Do you believe that you have what it takes to succeed? Let me tell you something. I don't know you. I don't know what your past was. I don't know where you came from. I don't know any about that. What I do know is that if you signed up for this program and you're willing to go the extra mile, if you're willing to invest in yourself and learn and grow yourself, as long as you're not selling something that's a dead-end sales job, dude, you're going to make it in this industry. You will because everyone who studies the straight line does. Bottom line, the system works as long as you apply it and work hard. So you're in the right place. So this is from Bryce. Question. If we feel like we have a general idea of the SLB, but want to take it to the next level, how do we really become a master with it? Well, Bryce, I tell you again, I have a program called Straight Line Certification. It's a very formal program. And the reason it's taking me a long time to launch is because I want this to be something that is so amazing and so airtight. And part of that is something called interactivity, meaning that in the old way of training online, you watch me on video and you take notes and then you implement here with this new straight line certification, which is actually a five tiered program. It's not just online. This in-person train with me directly. We have an event where actually you show up and I train you personally. But with the certification, every few minutes, I'll ask you a question about a topic that we're learning and you'll respond. You can say, yes, no, there's a number or something. And based on your response, there's a learning algorithm and it routes you to different contents. So it allows the train now to be fluid and interactive. When you do that, two things happen. Number one, it increases engagement because there's no one let me just talking while you listen. And I'm a very interesting person. I am. I try to be, make sure you use my tonality, keep you engaged. But when you start being able to interact with me, engagement goes through the roof. Second, it allows you to go through the training more quickly or more slowly based on what you need. Everyone's training is slightly different based on how you answer questions. So when they were in person, they did turn people off. Maybe they didn't look make eye contact the right way. Maybe they didn't shake hands, right? I mean, they stood too close. And because of that, they felt the disconnection with people. So they developed these limiting beliefs about themselves. They were actually correct. They sucked at it. But here's the thing. This is such an incredible marketer. We do a lot of stuff together. So the answer is yes, it's coming up shortly. So with the two-call system, do you always set up a follow-up appointment? And if you do, what's your process as they don't show up? So if you don't set up a follow-up appointment, by nature of a two-call system, you're setting up a second appointment, right? So here's the thing. There's two ways to do this, right? In a two-call system, here's why I can't answer this, because there's too many variables, right? There's two-call systems where the first call is on the phone, and then you follow up with an in-person appointment. There's other two-call systems where you call on the phone and you follow up with the phone. There's other ones where you're first one's in person, then you follow up in person, where you follow up on the phone. There's lots of variables here, okay? So I can't answer that. What I will tell you is I've struggled with back and forth, and the only way to figure this out is to test it in your industry is do you want to confirm an appointment with someone? And I always start off saying no. I do not want to confirm my appointments, because if someone doesn't show up, then they're going to feel that bad about them. They'll owe me, that sets me up for the next call. But if your cancellation rate is really, really high, then I would start confirming. You have to test this stuff. I choose not to confirm, because it gives someone a chance to say, oh, no, I'll cancel. If you get someone, here's what happens a lot with it. And this is, let's say, two-call over the phone, where you speak to someone on Monday, set up a call for Friday, but they forgot by Friday. And you call them, either you'll catch them or you won't. If you catch them, they'll take the call. Or if they have to reschedule, then they'll reschedule if you're bad, you'll get in the next time. So I choose not to do the day before the check, because they'll say, oh, no, it's bad, let's reschedule. Too many cancellations that way. So I would start off not doing a call the day before. But if you've got to drive eight hours somewhere, well, I'd definitely rethink that. So there's been a lot of chicken involved as well. Girls are not as forgiving as guys, right? One more question. Well, man, listen, some of that function is marketing. How do you get people to stop at your booth, right? That's one time. There's no difference whether you're selling in a convention center or in someone's door. The same rules still apply, all right? It's in those first couple of seconds when they lay eyes on you and they hear your voice, they're going to make the decision. Is this a person worth listening to or not? The difference is you've got to be that much better because there's a lot of other options beside you versus when you're at someone's door or you're the only game in town for that moment here. It's not like that. So again, brush up on your skill sets, okay? Make sure you have your presentation, especially your first five or 10 seconds. I want that scripted. Make up unconscious communication. Your words speak to the person's conscious mind. Your tonality and body language speak to their unconscious, their emotional mind.
